elements.sent-2411-001: This paper introduces a new approach for estimating core inflation indicators based on common factors across a broad range of price indices.
elements.sent-2411-002: Specifically, by utilizing procedures for detecting multiple regimes in high-dimensional factor models, we propose two types of core inflation indicators: one incorporating multiple structural breaks and another based on Markov switching.
elements.sent-2411-003: The structural breaks approach can eliminate revisions for past regimes, though it functions as an offline indicator, as real-time detection of breaks is not feasible with this method.
elements.sent-2411-004: On the other hand, the Markov switching approach can reduce revisions while being useful in real time, making it a simple and robust core inflation indicator suitable for real-time monitoring and as a short-term guide for monetary policy.
elements.sent-2411-005: Additionally, this approach allows us to estimate the probability of being in different inflationary regimes.
elements.sent-2411-006: To demonstrate the effectiveness of these indicators, we apply them to Canadian price data.
elements.sent-2411-007: To compare the real-time performance of the Markov switching approach to the benchmark model without regime-switching, we assess their abilities to forecast headline inflation and minimize revisions.
elements.sent-2411-008: We find that the Markov switching model delivers superior predictive accuracy and significantly reduces revisions during periods of substantial inflation changes.
elements.sent-2411-009: Hence, our findings suggest that accounting for time-varying factors and parameters enhances inflation signal accuracy and reduces data requirements, especially following sudden economic shifts.
elements.sent-2411-010: Inflation-targeting central banks use various types of core inflation indicators to asses inflationary pressure.
elements.sent-2411-011: Features that make these indicators useful include being robust to high-frequency volatility from transitory and sector-specific shocks, reliability in minimizing revisions of historical estimates, forecasting headline inflation in the short to medium-term, and being useful in real-time.
elements.sent-2411-012: The main objective of core inflation indicators are to provide a signal of underlying and overall inflation for a given economy.
elements.sent-2411-013: As a result, indicators with such features can serve as crucial tools for guiding short-term monetary policy decisions.
elements.sent-2411-014: Types of core inflation indicators include: CPI less the most volatile items, Weighted CPI, Median CPI, Trimmed CPI, and other model-based approaches.
elements.sent-2411-015: One of these model based approaches involve using factor models to extract the common component over many individual prices indices.
elements.sent-2411-016: These methods of obtaining a measure of underlying core inflation leverage high dimensional data sets and typically posses the ideal features of a core inflation indicator previously mentioned.
elements.sent-2411-017: For this reason, this paper focuses specifically on measures of underlying core inflation built using factor models.
elements.sent-2411-018: These models are used by various central banks to obtain a measure of core inflation.
elements.sent-2411-019: For example, the Bank of Canada (BoC) computes CPI-Common, as detailed in \cite{khan_common_2013 and \cite{khan_comprehensive_2015 .. The U.S.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Federal Reserve Bank of New York has two versions of their Underlying Inflation Gauge (UIG), as described in \cite{amstad_real_2009 and \cite{amstad_new_2017 , one using price data only while the other complements the same price data with other macro variables.}}

elements.sent-2411-022: However, it is important to acknowledge the inherent challenges associated with these indicators that are built using factor models.

elements.sent-2411-024: In most cases, when dealing with inflation, a single factor is assumed to be needed as it is associated with underlying inflation.
elements.sent-2411-025: However, other econometric procedures such as those described in \cite{bai_determining_2002 can also be used to determine the appropriate number of factors.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Due to revisions \cite{wynne_core_2008 criticizes the factor model-based approaches for measuring underlying inflation, but acknowledges that if historical revisions were found to be trivial then this criticism would lose power.}
elements.sent-2411-027: In the past, revisions were indeed typically negligible, which bolstered confidence in this approach.
elements.sent-2411-028: Yet, with the recent surge in inflation observed across various countries, these revisions have become considerably more concerning.

elements.sent-2411-030: Such cases have lead some central banks, including the BoC, to reconsider the use of such indicators (see \cite{macklem_whats_2022 ).}
elements.sent-2411-031: In this paper, we address the challenge of large revisions in core inflation estimates observed during changing inflationary regimes by modernizing the factor-based approach using recently proposed econometric procedures.
elements.sent-2411-032: Specifically, we propose two novel core inflation indicators that account for time-varying dynamics and multiple inflation regimes.
elements.sent-2411-033: First, we apply the methods developed by \cite{baltagi_estimating_2021 to detect structural breaks in high-dimensional factor models to develop an underlying core inflation indicator with structural change.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Second, we incorporate estimation methods for high-dimensional factor models with Markov switching, as proposed by \cite{urga_estimation_2024 , to develop an underlying core inflation indicator that adapts to regime shifts.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Prior research on total and trend inflation has shown compelling evidence for time-varying models (see \cite{stock_has_2002 ; \cite{perron_testing_2020 ; \cite{amisano_money_2013 ; \cite{stock_why_2007 ; \cite{stock_core_2016 ), supporting our argument that core inflation should also be modeled as subject to multiple regimes.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-037: Moreover, the Markov-switching model proves particularly useful in real-time.
elements.sent-2411-038: Our empirical application using Canadian price data supports the suggested advantages of these indicators.

elements.sent-2411-040: This is especially relevant for Canada, where our indicators directly address issues with the BoC’s CPI-Common identified by \cite{sullivan_examining_2022 concerning revisions.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Specifically, our findings suggest that the CPI-Common with Markov switching, which we call CPI-Common-MS, outperforms the basic CPI-Common in capturing inflation dynamics during periods of significant inflation fluctuation, whether inflation is rising sharply or beginning to stabilize.

elements.sent-2411-046: As a result, this paper contributes to the literature in four key ways.
elements.sent-2411-047: First, we introduce core inflation indicators that retain the beneficial attributes of traditional core inflation measures but are more robust to abrupt shifts in inflation.
elements.sent-2411-048: They also enhance underlying inflation signals and can serve as a short-term guide for monetary policy.
elements.sent-2411-049: Second, we contribute valuable insights to the ongoing debate over CPI-Common and factor model-based core inflation indicators by proposing avenues for improvement, thereby deepening the debate on core inflation measurement and its implications for effective monetary policy.
elements.sent-2411-050: Third, our CPI-Common-SC and CPI-Common-MS indicators perform particularly well for Canada and present themselves as suitable alternatives to the BoC’s CPI-Common.
elements.sent-2411-051: Finally, our methods can identify dates when underlying inflation, represented as the common factor among various price indices, undergoes regime changes.
elements.sent-2411-052: This has significant implications for inflation-targeting central banks facing elevated inflation levels.
elements.sent-2411-053: Notably, the Markov-switching approach offers the additional benefit of providing policymakers with a probabilistic statement about the state of inflation for a given economy.
elements.sent-2411-054: The next sections are structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-055: Section 2 briefly reviews core inflation indicators and outlines how to use a factor model approach to obtain estimates of underlying inflation.
elements.sent-2411-056: Section 3 introduces the core inflation indicators with multiple regimes that we propose here.
elements.sent-2411-057: Section 4 presents the empirical application using Canadian price data and introduces the CPI-Common-SC and CPI-Common-MS indicators that we propose as an alternative to the BoC's CPI-Common.
elements.sent-2411-058: Finally, section 5 provides concluding remarks and some directions for future research.
elements.sent-2411-059: Inflation-targeting central banks utilize various core inflation indicators to gauge underlying inflation trends, aiming to filter out short-term volatility caused by transitory or sector-specific shocks.
elements.sent-2411-060: The purpose of core inflation measures is to provide a stable, reliable signal of persistent inflationary pressure, which is crucial for guiding monetary policy in the short term.
elements.sent-2411-061: Methodologies to obtain core inflation measures can broadly be categorized into exclusion measures, factor models, filtering approaches, machine learning approaches, and non-parametric procedures, each offering a unique approach to identifying a signal of underlying inflation.
elements.sent-2411-062: The most common approaches used by central banks are exclusion measures and factor models, though machine learning approaches, such as the one proposed in \cite{phil_ml_coreinf , present an interesting avenue as they are specifically calibrated for optimal forecasting performance.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Exclusion-based indicators, such as CPI excluding energy or other volatile items, are straightforward and aim to eliminate specific components from the Consumer Price Index (CPI) that are known for high volatility.
elements.sent-2411-064: Such measures are simple to interpret but aren't always a reliable measure of underlying inflation when other sectors experience short-term shocks.
elements.sent-2411-065: Another subset within exclusion measures includes trimmed and median CPI indicators.
elements.sent-2411-066: These approaches remove extreme price changes, with the median approach focusing on the middle of the price distribution and trimming techniques excluding certain portions of the distribution tails.
elements.sent-2411-067: As highlighted by \cite{khan_core_2022 , trimmed and median CPI indicators offer robustness by not relying heavily on historical patterns, making them especially useful during periods of economic instability.}
elements.sent-2411-068: However, interpretability becomes more nuanced with these measures and by excluding certain items, these exclusion measures might even inadvertently ignore significant inflation trends.
elements.sent-2411-069: The factor model approach represent a prominent methodology for deriving a signal of underlying inflation and hence building a measure of core inflation.
elements.sent-2411-070: The approach involves estimating a common factor that captures the shared underlying trend among a large set of price indices.
elements.sent-2411-071: Static factor models, such as the Bank of Canada’s CPI-Common, extract these factors under the assumption that the common trend remains constant over time.
elements.sent-2411-072: In general, factor models can often provide better predictions of macroeconomic variables over short- to medium-term horizons (see \cite{stock2002macroeconomic ).}
elements.sent-2411-073: For measuring underlying inflation, they have shown to effectively identify persistent inflation drivers, which are less volatile and hence more stable indicators of future inflation, particularly when large datasets are available.
elements.sent-2411-074: However, during recent times of structural changes, we have seen some factor-based methods, like the BoC's CPI-common, be subject to large historical revisions, which is discussed in more detail below.
elements.sent-2411-075: For these reasons, policymakers will typically refer to both types of measures as each can shed different insight.
elements.sent-2411-076: However, we believe it is important to improve either type of measure when possible and hence the motivation for introducing an improved methodology for obtaining a core inflation indicator using the factor-based approach in the next section.

elements.sent-2411-200: In this section we present the empirical application with Canadian price data.
elements.sent-2411-201: Specifically, we consider the the BoC's CPI-Common and the price data used to compute it.
elements.sent-2411-202: The CPI-Common is the BoC's measure of core inflation that is built using the factor model approach.
elements.sent-2411-203: It is computed using the 55 components of the CPI.
elements.sent-2411-204: The full list can be found in the appendix of \cite{statistics_canada_bank_2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-205: These 55 series are adjusted to remove the effect of changes in indirect taxes, are expressed in year-over-year percentage changes, and are never revised.
elements.sent-2411-206: Here, we may also consider month-over-month percentage changes.
elements.sent-2411-207: However, as discussed in \cite{khan_common_2013 , the use of year-over-year inflation rates eliminates concerns regarding the use of non-seasonally adjusted data and so year-over-year inflation rates remains the main focus.}
elements.sent-2411-208: Additionally, seasonally adjusted data is often revised due to revision to the seasonal adjustment factors.
elements.sent-2411-209: Therefore, the use of non-seasonally adjusted Canadian price data is well suited for a proper real-time analysis.
elements.sent-2411-210: Before estimating the factor model, each series is standardized.
elements.sent-2411-211: Price data becomes available in January of 1989 and so year-over-year inflation can only begin as early as January 1990.
elements.sent-2411-212: Hence, for this application we consider data from January 1990 to December 2023.

elements.sent-2411-288: To do this, we estimate $\hat{\pi ^m_t$ using an expanding window approach, as if in real time, and then forecast headline inflation from 1-month ahead to 12-months ahead over the post-COVID period from January 2020 to December 2023.}
elements.sent-2411-289: This produces a forecasting sample, at each horizon, of $T_{f =48$ observations.}
elements.sent-2411-290: Additionally, we examine forecasting over an extended period that includes the Great Recession, from January 2007 to December 2023.
elements.sent-2411-291: In this case, we obtain a forecasting sample, at each horizon, of $T_{f =204$.}
elements.sent-2411-292: To evaluate forecast accuracy, we use the Root Mean Squared Forecasting Error (RMSFE), defined as: \begin{equation}RMSFE^m_h = \sqrt{\frac{\sum^{T_f}_{i} (\hat{\pi}^m_t - \pi^{HCPI}_{t+h})^2}{T_f}}\end{equation} To jointly evaluate all models, we apply the Model Confidence Set (MCS) approach proposed by \cite{Hansen-Lunde-Nason2011 .}
elements.sent-2411-293: This is a sequential test procedure that can be used to evaluate the forecasting performance of all models and determine a set of models, $\hat{\mathcal{M _{(1-\alpha)\%}$, that will contain the best model with a given level of confidence.}}
elements.sent-2411-294: A higher value of $\alpha$ enforces a stricter elimination rule, resulting in fewer models in $\hat{\mathcal{M _{(1-\alpha)\%}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-295: Table \ref{tab:forecasting presents the results of this forecasting exercise where the $T_{max}$ test statistic and a level of significant $\alpha=0.25$ was used to determine the MCS.}

elements.figure-2411-010: \caption{Headline CPI \& CPI-Common from January 1990 to December 2023} \includegraphics[width=0.90\textwidth]{figures/cad_SC_MS3_202312.pdf} \label{fig:cpicommon_sc_ms} 0.95\linewidth{ Notes: The time series include Headline CPI (black), CPI-Common (blue), the Bank of Canada’s time-invariant factor model, CPI-Common-M3 (cyan), using the Markov switching approach proposed here with $M=3$ regimes, and CPI-Common-SC (green), using the structural break approach proposed here.}

elements.sent-2411-340: Specifically, the CPI-Common-SC, which incorporates the structural breaks approach, and the CPI-Common-MS, which uses our Markov switching approach with $M=3$ regimes.
elements.sent-2411-341: We present all three indicators for the full sample, from January 1990 to December 2023.
elements.sent-2411-342: Both of our proposed measures are more stable throughout the sample, as their time-varying nature allows them to better capture the stability of inflation during periods when inflation is close to the target.
elements.sent-2411-343: Furthermore, both indicators track headline inflation more closely when it begins to deviate from the target.
elements.sent-2411-344: Compared to the CPI-Common-SC, the CPI-Common-MS once again presents a more optimistic view at the end of our sample, suggesting that inflation is now much closer to the Bank's target.
elements.sent-2411-345: This paper proposes two novel indicators for underlying core inflation that are robust to high frequency volatility from transitory and sector-specific shocks and to abrupt changes in inflation.
elements.sent-2411-346: This robustness to abrupt changes is a unique aspect of the indicators we propose, developed within a time-varying framework.
elements.sent-2411-347: The first indicator adopts a structural change approach, which is highly flexible but limited by its off-line nature.
elements.sent-2411-348: The second indicator is constructed using a Markov-switching framework, providing a real-time indicator of underlying core inflation that we argue can be useful as a short-term guide for monetary policy.
elements.sent-2411-349: To demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed indicators, we apply them to Canadian price data, proposing the CPI-Common-SC (based on structural change) and the CPI-Common-MS (using Markov switching) as potential substitutes for the BoC’s CPI-Common.
elements.sent-2411-350: Both methods succeed in eliminating or reducing revisions, an issue that has intensified amid recent inflation surge.
elements.sent-2411-351: These large CPI-Common revisions have cast doubt on the reliability of the current indicator, and our methods seek to address these challenges.
elements.sent-2411-352: Additionally, we evaluate the real-time performance of the CPI-Common-MS against a benchmark model without Markov switching, finding that the Markov-switching approach outperforms in terms of predictive accuracy for headline inflation over the short and medium term.
elements.sent-2411-353: Importantly, estimating the CPI-Common-MS also provides the probability of being in each regime at each point in time.
elements.sent-2411-354: Despite these advancements, there are important directions for future research.
elements.sent-2411-355: For the Markov-switching model, developing a formal hypothesis testing procedure is essential to determine the appropriate number of regimes at any given time, $t$.
elements.sent-2411-356: This could potentially lead to further improvements in the real-time performance of this indicator.
elements.sent-2411-357: Furthermore, additional research is needed to evaluate the stability of the number of factors across different regimes.
elements.sent-2411-358: Such investigations are currently in progress.
